stewart_read: Jamie initially confirms the address on file (123 West Avenue, Baltimore), then immediately volunteers a different address. This mismatch is the root cause of the entire DQ sequence. Bucky does not flag the discrepancy or ask a clarifying question — he simply asks for the new address. A single question here ('Is this a different address than the one on file?') would have surfaced the apartment situation earlier and with less momentum lost.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been deliberately letting Jamie self-correct to avoid seeming accusatory. Rejected — there is no evidence of a follow-up strategy, and the rep shows no awareness of the mismatch as a signal.
coaching_implication: When a customer volunteers a different address than the one on file, that is a qualification flag, not just a data-entry correction. Manager should walk Bucky through the verify beat with emphasis on address-mismatch as an early DQ signal — probe before recording the new address.

quote: Okay. So we don't do solar on apartments.
stewart_read: The DQ identification is correct. The execution is not. Bucky delivers the disqualifier with no empathy beat, no acknowledgment of Jamie's time, and no probe of whether the property has a qualifying structure. The codex's dq_rules flag non-homeowner as a hard DQ, but the exit protocol — how to leave the customer in a dignified, brand-positive state — is absent from Bucky's response.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been trying to exit quickly to avoid wasting time on a hard DQ. Rejected — the call then runs another 60 seconds of unproductive confusion, so speed was not achieved.
coaching_implication: Manager should establish a DQ exit template for Bucky: acknowledge the situation, thank the customer for their time, and close without competitor referrals. The absence of this template is the root cause of everything that follows in this call.

quote: So it also is a business.
stewart_read: Jamie's business framing is a distinct qualification signal from the rental house framing she offers 16 seconds later. Bucky does not engage with either. Whether Ion serves commercial properties is not addressed in the codex skeleton, but the rep's failure to ask a single follow-up question — 'Is this a business you own?' — means Ion exits without knowing whether a qualifying path existed. This is a knowledge gap: Bucky does not appear to know what to do with a property that is simultaneously an apartment, a business, and a rental house.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky simply did not know Ion's policy on commercial or mixed-use properties and defaulted to the apartment DQ. This is the more supported read — the rep shows no curiosity about the business framing.
coaching_implication: Manager should clarify Ion's policy on commercial and mixed-use properties for the floor. If Ion does not serve these, Bucky needs a clean one-line response. If Ion does serve them in some configurations, this call may have been a premature exit.

quote: Oh, who does it? Who does it?
stewart_read: Bucky's question 'Oh, who does it?' signals he has no DQ exit protocol and is now improvising. This is the moment the call's outcome is determined — not by the apartment DQ itself, but by the absence of a scripted or trained response to it. The question opens a door Bucky cannot close cleanly, and the Sunrun referral that follows is the direct consequence.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been trying to be helpful by acknowledging he did not know the answer. Rejected — the follow-up ('I have no idea') and the Sunrun referral confirm there was no strategy here.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to establish a floor-wide DQ exit protocol. The coaching point is not that Bucky said the wrong thing — it is that he had nothing to say and filled the silence with a competitor name. A simple 'I'm not the right person to point you to, but I appreciate your time' closes the call without brand damage.

stewart_read: Bucky volunteers Sunrun — a named competitor in Ion's competitive landscape — as a referral for a disqualified customer. This is not a cross-sell miss in the traditional sense (no Ion service was missed), but it is a competitive-intelligence failure: the rep actively routes a warm, motivated customer to a direct competitor with no strategic rationale. The codex flags Sunrun as a common competitor. Sending Jamie there is the opposite of brand preservation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been trying to build goodwill by being helpful, knowing the lead was dead for Ion. Rejected — goodwill built toward Sunrun does not benefit Ion, and the customer's subsequent request ('Could you call them for me?') shows the referral created dependency rather than goodwill.
coaching_implication: Manager should establish a clear floor policy: Ion reps do not refer disqualified customers to named competitors. If the manager wants a goodwill close for hard DQs, the language should be directional ('You'd want to look for a company that handles commercial or multi-unit properties') rather than naming a competitor. This is a brand-preservation coaching point, not a persuasion coaching point.

quote: It's also a rental house as well.
stewart_read: Jamie's second attempt to reframe the property — now as a rental house — is accompanied by a tone of increasing desperation ('I don't know who else she go to'). She is not just offering a qualification workaround; she is signaling that she has no other path and is relying on Bucky for help. Bucky does not acknowledge the emotional register of this moment. The empathy miss here is not about a vulnerability disclosure in the clinical sense, but about a customer who is visibly trying and being met with silence.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky may not have heard the emotional signal and was simply processing the property framing. This is the more supported read — the rep shows no awareness of Jamie's escalating distress.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to illustrate the difference between processing information and reading a customer. Jamie is not asking a technical question — she is asking for help. A single acknowledgment ('I hear you, let me make sure I understand what you have') would have changed the texture of the exit entirely, regardless of the DQ outcome.
